Glossary.Helpinfo:
For clarity when working specific techniques, pattern instructions may indicate the position of the carriage.

Commonly:
When the carriage is on the right, the row counter should indicate an even number.
When the carriage is on the left, the row counter should indicate an odd number.

Generally you start knitting with the carriage on the right and the row counter set to 000.

Is this a hard-and-fast rule? No... but this convention will help you keep track as you knit.

Glossary.Helpinfo:
Different models have different approaches to lace carriages:



Brother Lace Carriage
Brother lace carriage is used alternately with the main knitting carriage. Based on instruction from the patterning device (electronic or punchcard), the lace carriage selects and transfers stitches. The main carriage is then used to knit the row
Singer/Silver Lace Carriage
Silver/Singer lace carriages work independently of the main knitting carriage. The lace carriage, selects, transfers and knits the row.

Short Row Shaping (aka Partial knitting) is an ESSENTIAL skill for all machine knitters.

Partial knitting is just as it sounds ...you knit part of a row of knitting instead of the entire row. Instead of knitting just squares and rectangles, from bust darts, to curves to socks, short row shaping is used to shape your knitting.

Glossary.Example: Textured stitch where the working yarn passes under selected needles (slip)
Glossary.Helpinfo:
Slip Stitch in machine knitting refers to the process of missing stitches, allowing the working yarn to travel across the purl side of the knitting. These "floats" can be decorative when the purl side is used as the public side of the knitting.
The same process can be used to create multi-color slip. Needles are missed (slipped) and colors are changed, creating colorful, graphic designs.

Partial knitting is just as it sounds ...you knit part of a row of knitting instead of the entire row. Instead of knitting just squares and rectangles, from bust darts, to curves to socks, short row shaping is used to shape your knitting.

The same process can be used to create multi-color slip. Needles are missed (slipped) and colors are changed, creating colorful, graphic designs.

Glossary.Helpinfo:
Hung hems are a staple for machine knitters (especially for standard gauge machines) and they are a basis for many designer techniques.

They are a great alternative to ribbing.

  • Cast on and knit the depth of the hem
  • Knit a turning row
  • Knit the depth of the hem again
  • Re-hang the cast on edge to form the hem
  • Continue knitting

Glossary.Example: A technique that allows color changes in the middle of the row without having floats across the back of the knitting.
Glossary.Helpinfo:
Intarsia can be worked in different ways:
  • Manual:
    Using slip or hold, each color is worked with a single pass of the carriage. It will take multiple passes of the carriage to knit multiple colors across the row.
  • Built-in Intarsia Setting:
    Some machines can knit intarsia in one pass of the carriage with a carriage setting. Lay strands of yarn across the needles to be knit and pass the carriage, knitting multiple colors across the row.
  • Intarsia Carriage:
    Some machines offer an intarsia carriage accessory. This replaces the knitting carriage. Lay strands of yarn across the needles to be knit and pass the carriage, knitting multiple colors across the row.
No matter what method is used, it it important to twist the yarns where they join to prevent holes.

  • Built-in Intarsia Setting:
    Some machines can knit intarsia in one pass of the carriage with a carriage setting. Lay strands of yarn across the needles to be knit and pass the carriage, knitting multiple colors across the row.
  • Intarsia Carriage:
    Some machines offer an intarsia carriage accessory. This replaces the knitting carriage. Lay strands of yarn across the needles to be knit and pass the carriage, knitting multiple colors across the row.
No matter what method is used, it it important to twist the yarns where they join to prevent holes.

Glossary.Example: The distance between needles in mm
Glossary.Helpinfo:
What is needle pitch?
The gauge (pitch) of a knitting machine is determined by the distance between the needles.

If you are a hand knitter, think of the different machine gauges as different hand knitting needle sizes.

NOTE:
Although the machine pitch determines the yarn you can use, the actual stitch sizes (knitting gauge) is controlled with the tension dials on the machine.

In hand knitting terms, think of this as choosing a needle size within a RANGE of needles.

The pitch of your knitting machine determines the range. The tension dials determine the needle size.

Common Terms:
3.6mm Fine Gauge
4.5mm Standard Gauge
5mm Passap
6mm
6.5mm
7mm
Mid-Gauge
9mm Bulky-Gauge

Glossary.Example: A method often used by machine knitters to identify and compare yarn weights
Glossary.Helpinfo:
A yarn industry standard, using Yards Per Pound, helps compare yarn weights and determine what yarns are best for your knitting machine

No matter what put up (cones, balls skeins) you use being able to compare the weights in YPP is much more accurate and informative than the "generic" terms of fingering, DK, Worsted, 4 Ply or 8 Ply.

You may have already discovered that 2 balls of "DK Weight" in different fibers or from different manufacturers probably have different yards or meters per ball. One may have 550 yards and another may have 600 yards. If the balls weight the same, the thickness of the yarn itself will be different and they will knit at different gauges on your machine. Comparing DK to DK may not give you the results you want.


Use the Knit it Now "Balls to Cones" Conversion tool to compare yarns by converting their weights and yardage to YPP

Glossary.Example: Mixing 2 colors of yarn for a mottled or streaked appearance
Glossary.Helpinfo:
2 Yarn ends for marled fabric

Machine knitters, get creative with yarn and color!
  • Run 2 strands of contrasting (or complementary) colors together. The yarn will “automatically” create interesting stripes for a graphic design
  • Stack cones and run the lower end through the top cone, allowing the yarns to twist as they are fed in to your yarn feeder
  • Use a yarn twister and the stripes disappear and you get a marled fabric
This sample is full needle rib (all needles in work on both beds) in the beautiful Jaggerspun Superfine Merino.

What’s a yarn twister?
A yarn twister is a nifty device that looks similar to a yarn winder. It gives you the ability to twist multiple strands of yarn together.

Lots of options:
Twisting will blend colors and change the texture of the resulting yarn strand. You can twist a fancy or textured yarn with a plain yarn. Make thicker yarn and interesting yarn combinations.

You can find twisters in the used equipment market (often named Darhuma yarn twister) There are also new twisters available from fellow machine knitter, Kris Krafter

Glossary.Example: A knitting machine brand name.  Often these were the same machine, just packaged under a different name in different parts of the world.
Glossary.Helpinfo:
The Brother/Knit King machines are unique when compared to Silver/Singer/Studio machines (again same machines, different brand packaging)

In addition to different terminology, the positioning of buttons/levers/dials, and other differences, in general, the biggest difference between the 2 brands is in the automatic patterning (punchcard or electronics).

Brother/Knit King
When working in pattern, the Brother/Knit King machines pre-select needles in preparation for the patterning to happen on the following row.

For example:
Row 1: Knit the row and pre-select the needles
Row 2: Knit the row in pattern according to the currently selected needles AND pre-select for the next row.

Silver/Singer/Studio
When working in pattern, the Silver/Singer/Studio machines select the needles AND knit all in one pass of the carriage.

For example:
Row 1: Knit the row and "memorize" the pattern for the next row
Row 2: Knit the row according to the memorized instructions AND memorize the pattern for the next row.

There are other differences in the machines, but these basic needle selection schemes can trip up knitters who work with both brands.

Is one brand "better" than another? It depends on who you ask Most knitters have a preference and it often depends on which brand they started knitting with.

  • If the rulers are set to in/cm the vertical point of origin can be positioned at any point, making it easier to measure from that point

Glossary.Example: Perfect the shaping of sloped edges.
Glossary.Helpinfo:
When creating patterns in Standard Garment shaping, DesignaKnit calculates the smoothest shaping sequences for diagonal edges. If the shapes are modified in original pattern drafting, or you are designing your own shape you will need to use the Diagonals tool to achieve the best results.

The knitting instructions shaping of diagonal edges in knitting needs to take into consideration the shape of stitches and rows (the ratio between them).
The decision to use one method depends on your garment design and the knitting method you choose.

The DesignaKnit Manual contains excellent examples and tips for using this tool.

Glossary.Example: Choose the knitting method Bind Off (Cast off) or Hold for gentle slopes
Glossary.Helpinfo:
Instruct DesignaKnit to generate the correct shaping instructions based on the shaping method you will use in your knitting. This is especially helpful for shoulder shaping and round necklines.

TIP: Display the outline in xxxx stitches and rows
No adjustment for knitting method. Stitches are decreased EVERY ROW for a straighter line. Used for Cut 'n Sew shapes
On the carriage side: Bind/Cast off stitches EVERY OTHER ROW
On the carriage side: Bind/Cast off stitches EVERY OTHER ROW, but with one stitch retained for Bind/Cast off on the next row (for a smoother edge)
Manual Hold/Short row: Opposite the carriage stitches are placed in hold position EVERY OTHER ROW
Automatic Hold/Short row: One needle is retained for automatic wrap on EVERY OTHER ROW

The difference between the actual body measurement and the finished garment size.
Minimum Ease Comparison Chart

Do Knit it Now Patterns Include Ease?

Don't measure your body

Ease is the difference between the actual body measurement and the finished garment size.

Ease is a personal choice. The best way to establish your preferred ease is to measure a sweater that you like and duplicate those measurements for your finished measurements.

Knit it Now Patterns Calculate FINISHED GARMENT SIZE

  • Build the pattern with YOUR measurements

    Knit it Now patterns don't add ease or change your measurements

Glossary.Example: Sponge strip that holds the needles in position for knitting.
Glossary.Helpinfo:
• Other than cleaning and oiling your machine, the one essential maintenance item is the spongebar. This bar maintains the needle positions as you knit.
  • No matter how much you use your machine, the sponge will deteriorate over time.
  • A flat spongebar will cause any number of problems and is the first step in troubleshooting knitting problems.
  • A flat spongebar can potentially damage needles and carriage. Do not attempt to even push the carriage back and forth with a flat spongebar.

Silver/Singer machines have "Magic Cams" and Point Cams to create single motifs.

Glossary.Example: A "mock" lace, knit with one very fine thread/yarn and one thicker yarn.  Adding a very fine thread gives the appearance of lace without stitch transfers.
Glossary.Helpinfo:
Machines with Thread Lace settings:
With the appropriate Cam/Carriage settings, both threads will knit together (as in plaiting), but the fine yarn will knit singly and form "lace holes" with no floats.

This is a great alternative to transferred lace. The sample used a white sewing thread. A more "invisible" choice would be to use a transparent filament, like those used by quilters.

Machines without Thread Lace settings:
The appearance of thread lace can be created using a fine "thread" for the 2nd color of a fairlisle pattern. Unlike machines with a Thread Lace setting, floats will be formed as normal fairisle.

Knit it Now generates our patterns programmatically. Set in sleeves are tricky because of the curves in the sleeve cap. The math is complicated, not only to shape the cap, but to make sure it fits into the armhole.

In order to accurately perform the calculations there are 2 factors:
  • The sweater size, including both the upper arm and armhole depth
  • The stitch and row gauge
Occasionally, the relationship between these numbers just doesn't work with our formulas.
In these cases we display a warning message.

The system will generate the sleeve instructions, but they will be inaccurate.

For example:
The size and gauge of this pattern is causing the highlighted instruction.

It impacts 2 rows. If you ignore this instruction and knit the rest of the instructions to row 220, your sleeve cap will not be impacted.

However other sizes and gauges may cause a greater impact.

Try this:
Edit your dimensions slightly, re-build the pattern and examine the results.

When working short rows or partial knitting (manual wrap method). The knitting sequence to prevent holes:

  • Knit back to complete the short row shaping (2 rows knit)

Glossary.Helpinfo:
Knitting stitches are not square. With most stitch patterns stitches are wider than they are tall.
When seaming (or picking up) stitches to rows, you will not be joining 1 to 1.

It's common practice to leave a plain stitch along edges that will be seamed.
  1. Plain knitting:
    if you work Mattress Stitch
  2. Shaping:
    It's good practice to work increases and decreases at least 1 stitch from the edge, leaving a seaming stitch
  3. Pattern Knitting:
    Keep a single plain stitch at the edge of your knitting for easier seaming
  4. Ribbing:
    Alternating knit and purl stitches, maintaining the rib stitch pattern makes for a professional finish.

At 01:38 in the video we used slip stitch to create a crease by programming an electronic machine.
If you have a manual or punchcard machine try the following:

  1. Mark the needlebed to identify the center stitch

  2. Set the carriage to slip in ONE direction

  3. Manually pull out all needles on either side of the marked needle

  4. Keep the marked needle in working position

  5. Slip in one direction

  6. Continue for the length of the band

The Brother Pattern Programming Device was an early method of creating stitch patterns electronically to be used with the 900 series of machines.

The device was connected with cables to a TV. Once the design (black and white squares) was complete, the stitch pattern was transferred to an electronic machine.
Cartridges were used along with floppy disks.

Stitch patterns could be stored on floppy disks with the FB100 Floppy disk Drive to save space on a cartridge

Glossary.Example: Depending on the construction and fiber, some yarns will bias when knit. No amount of blocking will remove the bias.
Glossary.Helpinfo:
Bias in machine knitting refers to the tendency of some yarns to skew or stretch diagonally, especially after knitting. This is a natural characteristic of certain yarns and can’t always be corrected by blocking.

Here are some tips to try to overcome bias
  • Carry a matching sewing thread
  • Try Plaiting
  • Use a stitch pattern
  • Knit a knit and purl pattern with your ribber
  • Knit looser
  • Use the yarn for Knitweave
  • Stripe with another (not-biasing) yarn
  • Use the yarn for hand knitting or crochet

Glossary.Example: To help keep bands from "popping out" and laying flat against the body, it's common practice to "grade" the tension as you knit the band piece(s).
Glossary.Helpinfo:
Grading tension (for bands)
For example:
  1. Pick up and hang the neckline edge
  2. Start knitting the band at the band tension
  3. Gradually REDUCE the carriage tension (make smaller) and knit to the turning row
  4. Knit the turning row
  5. Gradually INCREASE the tension (make larger) until you are back to the band tension

Glossary.Example: Singer/Silver/Studio machines: Point cams determine the range of pattern knitting.
Glossary.Helpinfo:
On Singer/Silver/Studio machines, the point cams determine the range of pattern knitting. The devices slide along the back of the needlebed.
Pattern knitting only happens between 2 point cams.

  • When setting Point cams at both edges of the knitting, the pattern will be knitted across the entire row.

  • When knitting isolated motif, position the point cam on each side of where you want the motif to knit, keeping the other sections in plain knitting.

    • Point cams are used in conjunction with the Needle-1 Cam
    Page 19 of the Silver Reed 840 Operation manual

Glossary.Helpinfo:
Singer/Silver/Studio machines: Needle 1 Cam tells the machine where to start and finish the pattern repeat. It is used in conjunction with the Point Cams to position the stitch pattern.

The pegs of the cam fit into the small holes at the back of the needlebed.
Usually the cam is place in the center of the bed. when knitting a single motif, position the needle 1 cam at either the left or right Point Cam.
You must put the Needle-1 Cam WITHIN the 2 Point Cams or at either Point Cam. Don't put it outside the Point Cams



NOTE N-1 Cams are NOT used with DesignaKnit

Glossary.Example: Increase/Decrease evenly across the row
Glossary.Helpinfo:
There are times when you are instructed to increase or decrease evenly across a row. This instruction could be used for creating gathers or reducing a large number of stitches in work.

Unlike in hand knitting, machine knitters need to remove the knitting from the machine, then rehang the stitches accordingly.
Stitches can be removed with a garter bar, waste knitting or a hand knitting needle.

  • Increasing: After removing the stitches from the machine, pull out the desired number of needles and rehang the stitches, skipping needles. Fill in the empty needles with the purl strand from the row below, or leave the needles empty for eyelets, increasing the number of stitches in work.

  • Decreasing: After removing the stitches from the machine, pull out the desired number of needles and rehang the stitches, doubling up stitches on needles evenly across the row, reducing the number of stitches in work.

Glossary.Example: As we machine knit, the knitting is stretched across the needlebed and we add weights. Dressing a swatch will give you a much more accurate gauge measurement and it will also account for any changes when you steam and/or wash your knitting.
Glossary.Helpinfo:
Dressing a swatch refers to the process of establishing the final condition of the knitting after resting, steaming and/or washing.
Knitting straight off the machine is not representative of how the knitting is going to feel, look, drape or measure in the finished garment. Dressing a swatch:
  1. Remove the knitting from the machine
  2. Tube the knitting (roll it lengthwise and pull to "pop" the stitches)
  3. Let it rest (at least overnight)
  4. Wash and/or steam the swatch as you are going to treat the final garment
  5. Measure and calculate your gauge
Once you've dressed the swatch you will know if the yarn shrinks, stretches, fades or biases. You'll be able to confidently knit your garment without any surprises.

Glossary.Example: A "mock" lace, knit with one very fine thread/yarn and one thicker yarn.  Adding a very fine thread gives the appearance of lace without stitch transfers.
Glossary.Helpinfo:
Machines with Thread Lace settings:
With the appropriate Cam/Carriage settings, both threads will knit together (as in plaiting), but the fine yarn will knit singly and form "lace holes" with no floats.

This is a great alternative to transferred lace. The sample used a white sewing thread. A more "invisible" choice would be to use a transparent filament, like those used by quilters.

Machines without Thread Lace settings:
The appearance of thread lace can be created using a fine "thread" for the 2nd color of a fairlisle pattern. Unlike machines with a Thread Lace setting, floats will be formed as normal fairisle.

Glossary.Example: Main bed carriage component that "reads" and "memorizes" punchcard instructions. (Silver, Singer, Studio)
Glossary.Helpinfo:
This is the heart of automatic patterning on a Silver/Singer/Studio punchcard machine. As the carriage passes the punchcard mechanism, the Memory Drums record the position of the holes in the card. On the return pass of the carriage, the appropriate needles are selected and positioned according to the Cam Setting (stitch technique).

NOTE: Often on machines that have been stored for a long time the memory drums can be locked up. The drum should turn freely for stitch patterning to occur.


NOTE: The memory drum "remembers" previous positioning even if you remove the punchcard. To clear the memory drum memory, remove the punchcard and leave the mechanism engaged. Pass the carriage a few times to reset it, then cancel patterning.

This is a Silver Reed/Studio term for use with the lace carriage.
Fashion lace is the opposite of "Simple Lace" where the single stitch transfers per row are all in the same direction. The stitches are transferred AND KNIT with the same pass of the carriage.
This is controlled by the carriage settings. "P" for Fashion Lace and "L" for Simple Lace.

Glossary.Example:
This is a Singer/Silver term referring to single stitch transfers, occurring in the same direction on each row.
This arrangement requires only 1 pass of the lace carriage to transfer the stitches

Glossary.Example: A 4.5mm machine accessory that transfers groups of stitches between beds.
When casting on in ribbing, a popular method is a "tubular" cast on.  This is similar to a Kitchener Cast On in Hand Knitting.
Glossary.Helpinfo:
A tubular cast on employs a number of very specific steps, including slipping stitches alternately on the ribber and main bed. The edge is "rounded" and similar to a tube.

One method of creating this beautiful cast on is with a "broken toe" method. Use this method to prevent the cast on edge from flairing and ruffling.

  • KCII: End needles follow the stitch pattern

Glossary.Example: The turn mark is a position on the needlebed where the carriage is "instructed" by the automatic patterning information
Glossary.Helpinfo:
Brother Electronic machines: This is a sensor in the machine that triggers the stitch pattern data being communicated with the carriage.

When setting up the stitch pattern, by passing the carriage past the turn mark, it will correctly select the needles of the current row of the pattern.

Once the pattern is established, it is NOT necessary to move the carriage past the turn mark on subsequent rows.

Glossary.Example: Punchcard or electronic mechanism that "automatically" selects needles to form stitch patterns (instead of manually pulling needles)
Glossary.Helpinfo:
One of the many advantages of machine knitting is the ability to quickly and easily knit in pattern.

Textured stitch patterns, multi-color patterns can all be quickly produced with the help of automatic patterning devices.

Using Punchcards or electronics, the machine automatically selects needles for pattern knitting like Tuck, Slip, Fairisle, Lace etc.

Machined that do not have punchcard or electronics (or non-working mechanisms) are referred to as "Manual Machines"

The term Mosaic knitting was coined by Barbara Walker for hand knitters.
The patterns are composed of narrow vertical and horizontal stripes that intersect, which resemble complex mazes.

For machine knitters, this technique can be accomplished with the techniques multi-color slip or multi-color tuck, both on the single bed and with the ribber.

Glossary.Example: Having the knitted piece smaller than the body size for a snug fit
Glossary.Helpinfo:
Ease is the difference between the actual body measurement and the finished garment size
.

Negative ease indicates that the knitted piece is smaller than the body measurement. This is often used for socks and hats that require a snug fit.

Ease is a personal choice. The best way to establish your preferred ease is to measure a knitted item that you like and duplicate those measurements for your finished measurements.

Knit it Now patterns are calculated based on the dimensions you enter. If you enter 40" for the bust and you've included ease, the finished sweater will be 40", including any ease you added. (We do not add additional ease to your measurements)

Glossary.Example: When partial knitting (short rows) wrap an adjacent needle to prevent holes
Glossary.Helpinfo:
When working short rows or partial knitting (manual wrap method). The knitting sequence to prevent holes:
  • Opposite the carriage place needles in hold (carriage set to hold)
  • Knit across the row
  • Wrap the first needle in hold to prevent a hole
  • Knit back to complete the short row shaping (2 rows knit)

An alternative to manual wrap is to Automatic Wrap.

Glossary.Example: Specialty ball winder to ply(twist) 2 (or more) strands into 1 strand.
Glossary.Helpinfo:
Machine knitters, get creative with yarn and color!

Use 2 (or more) thin yarns and make a unique yarn.

1 Run 2 strands of contrasting (or complementary) colors together as you knit.  The yarn will "automatically" create interesting stripes for a graphic design

2Use a yarn twister and the stripes disappear and you get a marled fabric


What's a yarn twister?
A yarn twister is a nifty device that looks similar to a yarn ball winder . It gives you the ability to twist multiple strands of yarn together. It helps to create a smoother, stronger, and more consistent yarn.

  • Twisting will blend colors and change the texture of the resulting yarn strand.
  • You can twist a fancy or textured yarn with a plain yarn.
  • Make thicker yarn and interesting color combinations.
  • Use very fine yarns that may not be suitable to knit singly on the machine
You can find twisters in the used equipment market. Search for Daruma yarn twister

This sample is knitted in full-needle rib (all needles in work) with the beautiful Jaggerspun Superfine Merino 2/18

Glossary.Example: The distance between needles in mm
Glossary.Helpinfo:
Do you need yarn on cones for machine knitting?
NO! All you need is to have the yarn wound in a way that will easily feed into the machine.
  • Use a center-pull ball from a yarn manufacturer
  • Rewind skeins or hanks with a ball winder
What weight for your machine?
For success with your knitting machine, you need to make sure you are using a yarn that is the appropriate weight for your machine.

If you are just getting started, use a medium weight yarn that fits in the hooks of the needles. With experience, you can experiment with different weights.

Machines come in different gauges. Gauge (Needle Pitch) refers to the distance between the needles and the size of the hooks of the needles.

Pitch: the distance between the needles (in mm)
What is needle pitch?
The gauge (pitch) of a knitting machine is determined by the distance between the needles.

If you are a hand knitter, think of the different machine gauges as different hand knitting needle sizes.

NOTE:
Although the machine pitch determines the yarn you can use, the actual stitch sizes (knitting gauge) is controlled with the tension dials on the machine.

In hand knitting terms, think of this as choosing a needle size within a RANGE of needles.

The pitch of your knitting machine determines the range. The tension dials determine the needle size.

The sleeve/armhole seams are often knit with decorative increases or decreases as a design feature.
An advantage of raglans over other styles is the fit. Because there are no shoulder seams to align with the shoulders they appear to fit a wider range of body types - wide or narrow shoulders.

Many knitters (both hand and machine) enjoy knitting raglan sweaters from the top down.

Glossary.Example: Used to help yarn flow more smoothly and form stitches correctly
Glossary.Helpinfo:
Yarn on cones that was wound to be used in a factory is often waxed. This allows for the speed of industrial knitting.

Many machine knitters wax their yarn either as they knit or as they rewind balls/skeins/hangs into cakes. Some knitting machines come with a small bit of wax and ledge on the mast to position it for ease of waxing as you knit.

Waxing yarn is a matter of personal preference.

You may use wax for every knitting project. Some knitters (including me) rarely think about waxing.

  • If you are using old, vintage yarn that has been around for years and is dry and full of static, consider wax.
  • If your machine is not knit knitting well, the yarn is breaking, tangling or stitches are not forming correctly, consider wax.
  • If you are stuggling with static, consider wax.
You don't need anything fancy for wax, just a chunk of paraffin or a white candle works.

Visit our blog to share your opinion about waxing yarn.

Glossary.Example: Connect a Silver Reed Electronic machine with Designaknit via USB.
Glossary.Helpinfo:
Unlike Brother machines, the Silver machines do not have any internal electronics. The SilverLINK Box translates data from DesignaKnit into a form that the knitting carriage can understand.
The carriage is connected to the SilverLINK box with a Curl Cord (Silver Reed) .

  • Stitch pattern is created in DAK on your computer (equivalent to holes in a punchard)
  • The data from your computer is sent to the SilverLINK box via the USB cable
  • The box "translates" the data into a format the carriage can understand
  • The data is sent to the carriage via the curl cord row by row
  • The data instructs the carriage to select and de-select needles
  • This instruction in combination with the carriage settings (tuck, slip, fairisle, etc) creates stitch patterns in your knitting

This device is used on all 800 series Silver Reed machines. The curl cord is required (older models may not have the curl cord). The SilverLink 5 connects via the curl cord to the main carriage, lace carriage, or the AG50 intarsia carriage. It uses a USB port connection to computers. It can also be used on the Knitmaster/Studio(US) SK550, SK560, and SK580 machines.

Glossary.Example: When you aren't sure if you have enough yarn and start a project anyways, playing "chicken" to the very end.
Glossary.Helpinfo:
We've all done it ... we're not sure if we have enough yarn and get down to the last few yards. Yarn Chicken causes more grey hair than dropped stitches.

Tip 1

Weigh and measure your swatch BEFORE you start knitting. Use the Knit it Now Yarn Estimator to determine if you have enough.


Tip 2

If you only have 1-2 rows left to knit:
A row of stockinette takes about 3 times the width of the knitting.
Measure what you have left against the knitting on the machine.

Glossary.Example: Yarn that is wound in a "circle" then carefully twisted for future use.
Glossary.Helpinfo:
To use yarn that is put up in a hank, carefully untwist it, remove the securing strands and open it into a circle.

Employ the arms of a friend to hold the circle open while the yarn is being wound into a ball
~or~
position the circle of yarn around a yarn swift to easily wind the yarn into a ball or cake.

Glossary.Example: Keep track of the needles in work by marking the needlebed instead of recording needle numbers
Glossary.Helpinfo:
If you've removed your knitting and need to rehang it on the same needles, it's often easier to mark the needlebed instead of recording the needle numbers.

Use a soft pencil or a water based marker.
It will be immediately obvious what needles to use.

This can also be used when hand manipulating stitches. For example, mark the needles used for cables instead of counting.

Glossary.Example: Fibers spun into single strands that are often twisted together to make multiple-ply yarns.
Glossary.Helpinfo:
To make yarn, fibers (wool, alpaca, cotton, bamboo etc) are spun into long strands (ply). These strands are then spun (twisted) together to form yarn. Single plys can be very fine (lace weight) to very thick (Islandic Lopi). Various yarns can be made of 2 - 20 plys. These yarns can be referred to as 2-ply, 4 ply, etc.

Using plys to describe yarn weights is still used in some parts of the world, but the meaning isn't always clear. The terms like "4-ply" or "8-ply" are used to describe the weight category of the yarn, not the actual number of strands.

In other parts of the world the terms lace, fingering, DK, worsted, or bulky, are used to describe the yarn's thickness

Glossary.MetaTitle: Ply – Machine Knitting Term
Glossary.Metadescription: In machine knitting, 'ply' refers to the number of individual strands twisted together to form a single yarn, affecting thickness and strength.

Glossary.Example: The term ‘yarn weight’ refers to the thickness of the thread, not the weight of the ball
Glossary.Helpinfo: The term "yarn weight" refers to the thickness of the yarn strand, not its actual weight or the weight of the ball. Since fibers vary in density, a yard of fluffy aran wool might weigh less than a yarn of 4-ply cotton, even though aran is considered the "heavier" yarn.

Glossary.Metadescription: Yarn weight refers to the thickness of yarn, crucial in machine knitting for determining gauge, stitch definition, and suitability for various projects.

Glossary.Example: Multiple of 6 rows plus 2
Glossary.Helpinfo:
A row multiple is the number of rows needed to complete one vertical repeat of a stitch pattern. It’s how often the pattern repeats as you knit up the fabric.

For example:
A stitch pattern with a row multiple of 4 means the pattern repeats every 4 rows. To keep the design consistent, you’ll work in groups of 4 rows.

Sometimes, patterns include extra rows for balance or symmetry. For instance, if it says "Row multiple of 4 + 2," you’ll repeat the 4-row pattern and then add 2 more rows to finish the design.

Knowing the row multiple is key when planning the height of your project or adding stitch patterns.

Row multiples are use when knitting many stitch patterns including cables.

Glossary.Example: Device for creating balls (or cakes) of yarn for easier machine knitting.
Glossary.Helpinfo:
As machine knitters, it's important that the yarn is "put up" so it can easily pass through the yarn tensioner for knitting.

Yarn that is put up in skeins and hanks cannot be used for machine knitting unless it is wound on a cone or in balls (or cakes).

Many machine knitters will also re-wind yarn into balls and check for knots before using it for machine knitting.

Glossary.Example: Carriage on the Right
Glossary.Helpinfo:
Vintage patterns will indicate that knitting machine's carriage is positioned on the right side of the needle bed, ready to move across the needles to knit the next row.

For clarity when working specific techniques, pattern instructions may indicate the position of the carriage.

Commonly:

When the carriage is on the right, the row counter should indicate an even number.
When the carriage is on the left, the row counter should indicate an odd number.

Generally you start knitting with the carriage on the right and the row counter set to 000.

Is this a hard-and-fast rule? No... but this convention will help you keep track as you knit.

Glossary.Metadescription: In machine knitting, 'COR' stands for 'Carriage on Right,' indicating the position of the carriage at the start of a row, which affects the direction of knitting and pattern formation.

Glossary.Example: Carriage on the left
Glossary.Helpinfo:
Vintage patterns will indicate that knitting machine's carriage is positioned on the left side of the needle bed, ready to move across the needles to knit the next row.

For clarity when working specific techniques, pattern instructions may indicate the position of the carriage.

Commonly:

When the carriage is on the right, the row counter should indicate an even number.
When the carriage is on the left, the row counter should indicate an odd number.

Generally you start knitting with the carriage on the right and the row counter set to 000.

Is this a hard-and-fast rule? No... but this convention will help you keep track as you knit.

Glossary.Example: A sweater style that is drawn tight at the waist or hip
Glossary.Helpinfo:
Machine knit 2 ways:
  1. Increases
    • Knit the band to size and scrap off
    • Rehang the band, increasing to the needed number of stitches for the body
  2. Decreases (preferred)
    • Scrap on the body
    • Rehang the cast on edge, decreasing to the required band stitches

It can also be used to join other seams, cutting down on finishing time.

You'll often see bell sleeves on dresses, blouses, tunics, and sweaters with variations like layered or ruffled designs.

Glossary.Example: A tool for transferring stitches
Glossary.Helpinfo:
Not to be confused with a Garter Bar , a decker comb is a multi-prong tool similar to a Multi-Prong Transfer Tool used to transfer large numbers of stitches.

Garter Bar: Transfers stitches AND can be used to turn and rehang the stitches back on the needles
Decker Comb: Removes stitches from the needles and allows easier transfers (example: decreases across the row)
Glossary.MetaTitle: Decker Comb in Machine Knitting
Glossary.Metadescription: A decker comb is a multi-prong tool for transferring large numbers of stitches, aiding in stitch movement and decreases across a row

Glossary.Example: A classic sweater style
Glossary.Helpinfo:
A dolman sleeve starts with a wide armhole that extends from the bodice and tapers down to a narrower fit at the wrist. This style creates a relaxed, flowing look and gives plenty of room for movement. Dolman sleeves can be subtle or dramatic, with variations in length and fullness to suit different designs.

Because of our needlebed limitations, Dolman sweaters can be a challenge to knit. One suggestion: Knit the back and fronts sideways, scrapping on and leaving open stitches to pick up for the sleeves.

Glossary.Example: A French dart is a long, diagonal dart that typically starts low in the side seam—often near the waist or hip—and angles upward toward the bust point. It helps shape both the bust and waist, creating a more tailored and flattering silhouette.
Glossary.Helpinfo: From our Blog

Glossary.Example: [Stitch Symbol] Separate Weaving yarn floats over and under the needles, the yarn does not form stitches, but creates decorative floats on the purl side of the knitting.
Glossary.Helpinfo: Weaving on the knitting machine can produce beautifully textured fabric. Here's a fun way to use small amounts of novelty or luxury yarns on your machine knit projects.

Glossary.Example: [Stitch Symbol] Main Stitches in DesignaKnit Stitch Designer
Glossary.Helpinfo:
Knit and Purl Symbols cannot be removed from the Stitch Palette.
As smart symbols they will select and deselect depending on the the selected knitting method.

Glossary.Example: [Stitch Symbol] Creates texture by skipping knitting (yarn travels OVER the needles)
Glossary.Helpinfo: Textured stitch pattern where individual stitches do not knit and the working yarn travels OVER selected stitches for 1-4 rows.

Glossary.Example: [Stitch Symbol] Textured stitch where the working yarn passes under selected needles (slip)
Glossary.Helpinfo:
Slip Stitch in machine knitting refers to the process of missing stitches, allowing the working yarn to travel across the purl side of the knitting. These "floats" can be decorative when the purl side is used as the public side of the knitting.
The same process can be used to create Multi-color slip. Needles are missed (slipped) and colors are changed creating colorful, graphic designs.

Glossary.Example: [Stitch Symbol] Simulates eyelets without stitch transfers.
Glossary.Helpinfo:
Machines with Thread Lace settings:
With the appropriate Cam/Carriage settings, both threads will knit together (as in plaiting), but the fine yarn will knit singly and form "lace holes" with no floats.

Glossary.Example: [Stitch Symbol] Marks where an eyelet will form in full transfer lace
Glossary.Helpinfo: Similar to a [k2tog, yo] in hand knitting, transferring a single stitch to its neighboring needle will create a hole. Leaving the emptied needle in working position completes the eyelet.

Glossary.Example: [Stitch Symbol]  Similar to k2tog in hand knitting, A stitch is transfered so it is leaning to the right when viewing the knit (public) side of the knitting.
Glossary.Helpinfo:
Knit 2 together is a hand knitting term referring to positioning stitches to lean to the right. Used for decreases or creating eyelet lace.

k2tog can easily be worked on the knitting machine. The key is to understand (and keep straight) left and right leaning stitches.
This can be a challenge because machine knitters view our knitting from the purl side.

Glossary.Example: [Stitch Symbol] Similar to PSSO in hand knitting, A stitch is transfered so it is leaning to the left when viewing the knit (public) side of the knitting.
Glossary.Helpinfo:
Similar to PSSO in hand knitting.
PSSO can easily be worked on the knitting machine. It is used for decreases or creating eyelet lace. The key is to understand (and keep straight) left and right leaning stitches. This can be a challenge because machine knitters view our knitting from the purl side.

Glossary.Example: [Stitch Symbol] Partial stitch share (Brother machines only)
Glossary.Helpinfo:
Instead of transferring an entire stitch, forming an eyelet, Fine Lace transfers one leg of a stitch to the adjacent needle.
In other words, the stitch is shared between 2 needles. This creates an interesting texture without holes.

Glossary.Example: [Stitch Symbol]  Switches knitting technique at defined point
Glossary.Helpinfo: Unless you say otherwise, DesignaKnit assumes the technique from the last pattern band keeps going through any plain rows—right up until it sees another Smart Symbol or a Technique Change Control symbol.

Glossary.Example: Status assigned to a smart symbol to control the position of needles for knitting stitch patterns.
Glossary.Helpinfo:
Setting needles so the machine recognizes them for patterning on the next row. Depending on your machine, this might mean they’re brought out to a certain position or highlighted by the software.
You can change the needle selection status for each color yourself—but the symbols are controlled by DesignaKnit.

Glossary.Example: Status assigned to a smart symbol to control the position of needles for knitting stitch patterns.
Glossary.Helpinfo:
Setting needles so the machine recognizes them for patterning on the next row. Depending on your machine, this might mean they’re brought out to a certain position or highlighted by the software.
You can change the needle selection status for each color yourself—but the symbols are controlled by DesignaKnit.

Glossary.Example: A motorized accessory to form both knit and purl stitches across a row. Brother standard gauge machines only | G-Carriage | Garter Carriage
Glossary.Helpinfo:
Also known as "the turtle" (because it's slow) this fabulous accessory can add even more design opportunities for Brother standard gauge knitters.
This accessory has also called Automatic Knitting Carriage, G-Carriage and Garter Carriage.
Glossary.MetaTitle: Automatic Knitting Carriage – Machine Knitting Accessory
Glossary.Metadescription: A garter carriage is a motorized accessory for knitting machines that automates the creation of garter stitch patterns, simplifying the process of producing textured fabrics.

Glossary.Example: A machine knitting technique where only selected needles are knit while others are held. Used for shaping, buttonholes, cables, and decorative effects by building up stitches in one area while skipping over others.
Glossary.Helpinfo:
The term "bridging" was coined by long-time machine knitter Susan Guagliumi, know for her hand manipulated techniques on the machine.

The basis of bridging is short row shaping or Partial Knitting

Glossary.Helpinfo: Machine knitting instructions may refer to specific needles or groups of needles. The convention is to refer to the needles based on their location in relation to 0 on the needlebed.

Glossary.Example: Bind off needles 1-10 left of Zero
Glossary.Helpinfo:
The “zero needle” is not a needle. It's the reference point at the very center of your knitting machine’s needle bed. It’s essential for aligning patterns, centering your work, and coordinating with a ribber.

It's often referred to in knitting instruction as "10-1 left" of Zero or "1-10 right of zero"

Zero on the needlebed
Glossary.Image_description: Needlebed zero point demonstration

Glossary.Metadescription: The zero needle is the center reference point on a knitting machine’s needle bed. Learn why marking true zero is essential for aligning patterns, centering projects, and using a ribber
